content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Part 5 of the “Frontend Performance That Users Feel” series. Your server-rendered page looks instant — and does nothing when tapped for two more seconds.
Server-side rendering was supposed to fix slow JavaScript apps. The server sends fully-rendered HTML, the user sees content immediately, everyone’s happy. Except for a strange gap: the page appears in 0.5 seconds but doesn’t respond to clicks for another 2 seconds. Buttons look ready but do nothing. Users tap, nothing happens, they tap again.
That gap is hydration — the step where the browser downloads your JavaScript and “wakes up” the static HTML into an interactive app. It’s the hidden tax of modern SSR frameworks, and understanding it is the difference between a page that’s fast-looking and one that’s actually fast. Let me explain it and the modern ways around it.
Server rendering makes content appear fast. Hydration is the bill that comes due to make it work — and during that bill, your beautiful page is a screenshot that ignores the user.
What Hydration Actually Is
With SSR, two things happen:

• The server renders your components to HTML and sends it. The user sees the page (fast LCP — Part 4).

• The browser downloads the same component code, re-runs it, and attaches event listeners to the existing HTML so it becomes interactive. This is hydration.
The catch: step 2 has to download and execute roughly your entire JavaScript bundle before anything is interactive — and it re-does work the server already did. So the more components on the page, the longer the gap between “looks ready” and “is ready.” You paid to render twice: once on the server, once in the browser.
Server renders HTML → user sees page (0.5s)
↓ [dead zone: looks ready, ignores taps]
Browser downloads + runs JS → page hydrates → actually interactive (2.5s)That dead zone is what users feel as “laggy” even though the page painted instantly.
Fix #1: Ship Less JavaScript to Hydrate
Hydration cost is proportional to how much JS must run. Everything from the bundle article (Part 1) applies directly:

• Code-split so each page hydrates only its own components, not the whole app.

• Cut dependencies — less code means faster hydration.

• Lazy-load below-the-fold interactivity so the initial hydration is small.
The cheapest hydration is the JavaScript you didn’t send. Before reaching for clever techniques, just ship less.
Fix #2: Don’t Hydrate What Doesn’t Need It (Islands)
Most of a page is static — headers, text, footers, marketing copy — and doesn’t need any JavaScript at all. The islands architecture hydrates only the genuinely interactive parts (a search box, a cart widget) and leaves the rest as plain HTML:
[ static header ] ← no JS, never hydrates
[ static article text ] ← no JS, never hydrates
[ interactive comment box ] ← an "island" — only this hydrates
[ static footer ] ← no JSFrameworks like Astro (and Qwik, Fresh) make this the default. Instead of hydrating an entire page, you hydrate three small islands, and the dead zone nearly disappears because there’s barely anything to wake up.
Fix #3: Stream and Prioritize (Modern SSR)
Newer frameworks (React Server Components, Next.js App Router, SolidStart) attack the problem from another angle:

• Server Components render on the server and ship zero JavaScript to the client for the non-interactive parts — only the interactive components send code to hydrate.

• Streaming SSR sends HTML in chunks as it’s ready and hydrates progressively, so the important, above-the-fold interactive parts wake up first instead of waiting for the whole page.

• Selective/progressive hydration prioritizes what the user is most likely to interact with.
The common thread: stop treating the page as one monolithic blob that must fully hydrate at once. Break it up, send less client JS, and wake up the interactive parts in priority order.
Fix #4: Measure the Gap, Not Just the Paint
A great LCP can hide a terrible interactivity gap. The metric that catches this is INP (Part 4) and the older TTI — they measure when the page actually responds, not when it appears. Watch them:

• If LCP is great but INP is poor, you have a hydration problem — the page looks fast and acts slow.

• Test on a mid-range device where the JS execution gap is widest, not your fast laptop where hydration finishes almost instantly.
Putting It Together

• Ship less JS — code-split and trim deps so there’s less to hydrate.

• Hydrate only interactive parts — islands architecture for content-heavy pages.

• Use modern SSR — Server Components + streaming to send less client code and wake up in priority order.

• Measure interactivity (INP/TTI), not just paint, on real devices.
The Takeaway
Server-side rendering solved the blank-screen problem and accidentally created a subtler one: a page that looks finished while it’s still downloading the JavaScript needed to function. That hydration gap is invisible in a paint-time metric and obvious to a user tapping a dead button. The fix is to stop hydrating everything at once — send less client JavaScript, hydrate only the parts that are actually interactive, and let modern frameworks stream and prioritize. Close the gap between “looks ready” and “is ready,” and your fast-looking page finally becomes a fast-feeling one.

This is Part 5 — the finale of the Frontend Performance That Users Feel series. You’ve now got the set: shrink the bundle, tame re-renders, optimize images, fix the three Core Web Vitals, and close the hydration gap.
Have you ever shipped a page that looked instant but ignored taps for seconds? 👇
